# Research Narrator Summary: Market and Product Opportunities for Blueprint Genetics Oy

*To be generated by Research Worker in TASK-FINAL after all synthesis sections are complete.*

---

This file will contain a TTS-optimized executive summary of the research findings, written in flowing prose suitable for audio narration.

**Expected content:**

- Overview of genetic testing market trends and growth projections
- Blueprint Genetics' positioning in the hereditary disease diagnostics market
- Key competitive dynamics and differentiation opportunities
- Strategic recommendations for the 2026-2029 planning horizon
- Actionable next steps for product development and market expansion

---

*Target length: 2,000-3,500 words*
*Style: Conversational, no tables or bullet lists, all numbers spelled out*

There are also important safety mechanisms. If the AI fails at a task three times in a row, it marks that task as failed and moves on to the next one. This prevents infinite loops of retrying the same broken thing. The system also supports a stop signal, a simple file that when created tells the loop to shut down gracefully after finishing its current task. And there is a human checkpoint every ten iterations where the system asks the user whether it should keep going.

When we synthesize these two approaches, we get a unified model with five states. The first state is initialization, where the agent reads all its state files and figures out what to do next. The second state is planning, where it thinks through the task using the Hermes scratch pad approach. The third state is execution, where it actually does the work. The fourth state is verification, where it checks that the work was done correctly. And the fifth state is update, where it writes results to files and marks the task as complete. After the update, it loops back to initialization and starts the next task.

Now let us talk about how Visual Studio Code makes all of this possible. VS Code has a built-in system for customizing AI behavior through something called agent files. These are simple text files with a special extension that define an AI persona. Each agent file has two parts. The first part is a header written in YAML format that specifies the agent's name, what tools it can use, which other agents it can delegate to, and what AI model it should run on. The second part is the body, written in regular text, that provides detailed instructions for how the agent should behave.

VS Code also provides a three tier memory system. The first tier is called user memory, which is stored at a path called memories and persists forever across all projects and conversations. The first two hundred lines of user memory are automatically loaded into every conversation, so anything you put there will always be available. The second tier is session memory, stored at memories session, which only lasts for the current conversation and is cleared when the session ends. The third tier is repository memory, stored at memories repo, which is scoped to the current project. Repository memory has an important limitation: you can create files there, but you cannot update or delete them programmatically.

The memory system has strict guidelines. User memory must be extremely brief because it is loaded automatically. Think single line bullet points, not paragraphs. It should be organized by topic in separate files. And before creating any new memory, the agent should check what already exists to avoid duplicates.

Beyond memory, VS Code provides skills, which are reusable packages of instructions that the agent can load on demand. A skill is a directory containing a file called SKILL.md with instructions, plus optional supporting files. Skills load progressively in three levels. At the first level, only the name and description are read. At the second level, when the AI determines the skill is relevant to the current task, the full instructions are loaded. At the third level, any additional files referenced in the skill are loaded only when specifically needed. This progressive loading is important because it means you can have dozens of skills installed without wasting the AI's limited attention on irrelevant ones.

The most exciting part of the skill system is that agents can create new skills dynamically. There are four patterns for this. The first pattern is direct extraction: after solving a complex problem, the user asks the AI to save that workflow as a skill. The second pattern is memory to skill promotion: when the same insight appears in memory three or more times, it gets graduated to a formal skill. The third pattern, drawn from the Ralph technique, uses a helper agent to create or update skills without interrupting the main agent's work. The fourth pattern, from Hermes, involves systematically testing and improving skill instructions based on past performance.

The practical self improvement loop works like this. First, the agent detects a useful pattern during its work and saves a note in session memory. If the same pattern shows up again, it gets promoted to user memory. If it keeps showing up, a helper agent creates a formal skill from it. Finally, the promoted content gets cleaned out of user memory to stay under the two hundred line limit. This creates a natural cycle where temporary observations become permanent capabilities.

Let us now turn to how the task management system works in detail. The central coordination mechanism is a markdown checklist stored in a file called RESEARCH PROGRESS, or sometimes just PROGRESS. This is essentially a to-do list using standard checkbox syntax. An empty checkbox means a task is pending. A checkbox with a tilde means the task is currently being worked on. A checkbox with an X means the task is complete. A checkbox with an exclamation mark means the task failed after three attempts. And a checkbox with a B means the task is blocked by a dependency.

There is a critical rule that only one task should ever be marked as in progress at any given time. This enforces the Ralph principle of working on one thing at a time and prevents the AI from getting scattered.

The checklist supports two structural patterns. The flat pattern, used by Ralph, has three sections: tasks, completed, and blocked. When a task is done, it physically gets moved from the tasks section to the completed section. The phased pattern, which this research system uses, groups tasks by project phase and changes only the checkbox marker without moving lines around. The phased pattern is better for larger projects because it preserves the visual organization.

The key tool for updating this checklist is called replace string in file, which is a built-in VS Code tool. It works by finding an exact text string in a file and replacing it with a different string. For toggling a checkbox, the agent simply replaces the line containing the empty checkbox with the same line containing a filled checkbox. This tool has an important safety feature: if the text to be replaced appears more than once in the file, the operation fails entirely. This prevents accidental modifications to the wrong task.

An important property of this file based approach is that it supports what we call hot editing. The human can modify the checklist at any time, adding new tasks, removing tasks, changing priorities, or manually marking tasks complete. Because the AI reads the file fresh at the start of every loop, any human edits take effect immediately on the next iteration. There is no cached state that can get out of sync.

Now let me explain how the agents actually communicate with each other. VS Code provides a tool called run subagent that launches a separate AI agent to handle a specific subtask. The subagent runs in its own context window, completely isolated from the calling agent, and returns a single message when it is done. This is the primary way the Planner agent delegates work to the Worker agent.

The Planner sends a prompt to the Worker that includes the task identifier, the file paths it needs to read, and clear completion criteria. The Worker reads those files, does the research, writes findings to the synthesis file, marks the task complete in the progress file, and returns a brief summary. The Planner then reads the updated files to verify the work was done.

There is also a mechanism called handoffs, which provides transition buttons in the chat interface. After an agent finishes its response, handoff buttons appear that let you switch to a different agent with a pre-filled prompt. This is useful for human supervised workflows where someone wants to review output at each stage, but it is not suitable for fully autonomous operation because it requires the response to complete before the transition can happen.

The most robust communication mechanism, however, is simply the file system itself. All agents share the same workspace and can read and write to the same files. The progress file serves as the shared database. The synthesis file accumulates output. The activity log provides an audit trail. This file based approach works because it is persistent, auditable, human editable, and naturally prevents the need for complex inter-agent messaging protocols.

We verified that the complete Planner to Worker to Reviewer pipeline is fully feasible using VS Code's native capabilities. The Planner reads the task list and assigns work via run subagent. The Worker executes with full tool access and writes results to files. The Reviewer, which is restricted to read only tools, verifies the output and returns a pass or fail verdict. The only gap is that VS Code does not have a built-in mechanism for running an infinite loop. The workaround is the Ralph approach: the agent's instructions tell it to keep looping, and when the context window fills up, the human re-invokes the agent with a simple command like proceed. The agent reads the state files and picks up exactly where it left off.

So to summarize the full system, here is what gets deployed. You create three to four agent files in the project's agent directory. The Research Planner decomposes questions into tasks and creates the initial state files. The Research Worker executes tasks autonomously using the Ralph loop pattern. The Research Reviewer verifies output quality with read only access. And optionally, a Research Coordinator manages the full pipeline using nested subagents.

The file system layout includes the research brief, which defines objectives, the progress file which tracks task status, the guardrails file which sets quality standards, the synthesis file which accumulates findings, the sources file which registers all fetched URLs, and the activity log which records every action taken.

The self improvement pipeline works across sessions. Short term observations go to session memory. Recurring patterns get promoted to user memory. Frequently used patterns become formal skills. And the whole system stays lean by pruning promoted content from lower tiers.

For practical deployment, there are ten steps. Create the agent directory with the agent files. Create the initial brief and progress files, either manually or by running the Planner. Create the guardrails file with quality standards. Create empty output files for the synthesis, sources, and activity log. Enable the VS Code setting that allows subagents to create further subagents. Invoke the Planner agent with your research question. Review the generated files and make any adjustments. Invoke the Worker agent to begin the autonomous loop. Monitor progress through the progress file and activity log. And re-invoke the Worker whenever the context window fills up.

The system is designed to be simple, transparent, and resilient. Every piece of state lives in a readable text file. Every agent starts fresh each time. Every action is logged. The human can intervene at any point by editing any file. And the system gets smarter over time by converting successful patterns into reusable skills.

There are some important warnings to keep in mind. The two hundred line limit on auto loaded user memory means you must be selective about what gets stored there. Memory bloat will degrade performance. The three strike rule means some tasks will be marked as failed and need human attention. Subagent nesting is disabled by default and has a maximum depth of five when enabled. And all skill changes should be reviewed via version control diffs before being committed, to ensure the self improvement loop does not introduce drift in agent behavior.

This concludes the research summary. The core insight is that you do not need complex frameworks or external services to build a sophisticated autonomous research system. The combination of structured text files for state management, fresh context per iteration for reliability, progressive skill loading for efficiency, and simple file based communication for multi-agent coordination gives you everything you need. The system works today, using only the tools that come built into Visual Studio Code.
